---
title: "09 - It's Raining Functions"
author: "David Rach"
date: 04-14-2026
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    slide-number: true
    incremental: true
page-layout: full
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: true
---


![](/images/WebsiteBanner.png)

::: {style="text-align: right;"}
[![AGPL-3.0](https://img.shields.io/badge/license-AGPLv3-blue)](https://www.gnu.org/licenses/agpl-3.0.en.html) [![CC BY-SA 4.0](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-CC%20BY--SA%204.0-lightgrey.svg)](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/)
:::

For the YouTube livestream schedule, see [here](https://www.youtube.com/@cytometryinr)

For screen-shot slides, click [here](/course/09_Functions/slides.qmd)

<br>

---

# Background

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Welcome to the ninth week of the "Cytometry in R course". Over the last couple months, we have gradually built out a foundation of basic R skills, and explored some of the flow cytometry analysis infrastructure that we will be using throughout the course. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
In the process, we have extensively used functions, i.e., the tools within a toolbox, to carry out various useful tasks. We saw during [Week 01](/course/01_InstallingRPackages/) that when we install new packages, we gain access to their functions, enabling us to extend far beyond the boundaries of what the base R software is capable of by itself (as long as we remember to to attahc the package to our local environment (via the `library()` call) of course). 
:::
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
We also saw how just like tools in a toolbox, different functions take different [types](/course/03_InsideFCSFile/index.qmd#) of input objects, and the outputs we get back can be modified by providing additional arguments (typically show-in within ()). 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
While it is great that the R community as a whole has developed a small horde of useful functions, sometimes we need something that is not yet available. Today we will learn how to write our own functions, which will enable us to start exploring beyond the bounds of what others have already done. In the process, we will learn of a couple ways to iterate through vectors or lists, reducing the times we need to copy and paste the same of codes for slightly different inputs, and start to gain an appreciation of [functional programming](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Functional_programming) in the R context.
:::
:::

---

# Walk Through

## Functions

### Structure

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
At the most basic level, what makes up a function in R? Let's take a look at a simple example.
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
MyFirstFunction <- function(){
    message("Always spin-down your antibodies!")
}
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
The name of the function is defined by placing it on the left-side of the assignment arrow.  To the right we place the `function()`, which conveys to R that it will be creating a function. Although currently empty, individual function arguments that will make up the inputs are placed within the parenthesis (). And finally, the actual code being run by the function is palced within the curly brackets {}. 
:::
:::

---


::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
When we run this code-block, we end up creating a function. We can see this appear within our local environment, appearing under the variables tab in the right-secondary sidebar. 
:::
:::


::: {.fragment}
![](images/00_Functions.png)

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Once we have created a function that is present in our environment, we are able to run/use it. In the case of the first function example, no inputs or arguments are needed (as there is nothing between the ()). For output, all that is present within the {} is code to send a message to console, which we will get back if we run the function by calling its name in our console. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#| message: true
MyFirstFunction()
```

:::

---


### Arguments

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
In the case of our first function above, no input arguments were provided, meaning the function was entirely self-contained, allowing us to retrieve the code output by simply running the function name. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#| message: true
MyFirstFunction()
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Lets modify this initial self-contained argument to allow us to provide an input argument to change the output message. To modify our function to accept input arguments, we decide on our argument name, and place it within the parenthesis (). From there, we can modify our line of code within the {} to achieve our desired outcome.  
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
MySecondFunction <- function(BestFluorophore){
    message("Unquestionably, the best fluorophore is ", BestFluorophore)
}
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
And we can check to make sure it is running correctly similar to how we would for any function we might encounter, by running the line of code
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#| message: true
MySecondFunction(BestFluorophore = "APC")
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Lets continue our roll and see if we can provide multiple arguments to our function. We can achieve this by separating individual function arguments within the () with commas. Don't forget to also make sure the argument you specified within the () is also accounter for within the {}. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
MyThirdFunction <- function(BestFluorophore, WorstFluorophore){
    message("Unquestionably, the best fluorophore is ", BestFluorophore)
    message("And by far, the worst fluorophore is ", WorstFluorophore)
}
```

:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#| message: true
MyThirdFunction(BestFluorophore = "APC", WorstFluorophore = "PerCP-Cy5.5")
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Once we have a function set up that accepts multiple arguments, what happens if we forget to provide an argument? Let's intentionally forget "WorstFluorophore" and see. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#| error: TRUE
MyThirdFunction(BestFluorophore="APC")
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
As you see, we get back an error. The reason for this is that a function lives in relative isolation from the rest of our R session. It typically can only see what you provided as inputs, or what is spun up by the code within the function itself. 
:::
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
We can also provide default values for our given arguments. For example, we could modify to provide WorstFluorophore default by setting a value equal to it within the ()
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
MyFourthFunction <- function(BestFluorophore, WorstFluorophore="PerCP-Cy5.5"){
    message("Unquestionably, the best fluorophore is ", BestFluorophore)
    message("And by far, the worst fluorophore is ", WorstFluorophore)
}
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Which would then allow for the partially incomplete function to fall back on the default argument. 

:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#| message: true
MyFourthFunction(BestFluorophore="APC")
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
However, you are not locked in on your default option, as if you provide an actual argument, it will take preference over the provided default. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#| message: true
MyFourthFunction(BestFluorophore="APC", WorstFluorophore="Zombie Aqua")
```

:::

---


## Iteration

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Recalling back to [Week 02](/course/02_FilePaths/index.qmd), we ended up creating our first vector, which contained a list of fluorophores.
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
Fluorophores <- c("BV421", "FITC", "PE", "APC")
str(Fluorophores)
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Individual fluorophores from this vector can be provided as arguments to our function, by specifying their index location within the vector. For example
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#| message: true
MySecondFunction(BestFluorophore = Fluorophores[1])
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
If we wanted to check the output message for each item in our vector, one approach would be to copy-and-paste the line of code, and swap out the vector index position for each new line. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#| message: true
MySecondFunction(BestFluorophore = Fluorophores[2])
```

:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#| message: true
MySecondFunction(BestFluorophore = Fluorophores[3])
```

:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#| message: true
MySecondFunction(BestFluorophore = Fluorophores[4])
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
While achievable, this ends up producing several lines of code. As you get further into the course, and more code is written to tidy individual dataframes, generate individual plots, having to copy-and-paste repeated blocks of text that only change based on the input values can become a chore. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
For this reason, there are a couple ways that we can sequentially pass through the objects within a vector or list to our function of interest, and retrieve the outputs that result. 
:::
:::

---

### For-loops

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
A for-loop is one way to iterate through a vector. The syntax can look quite startling when encountered for a first time. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#| eval: FALSE
for (Name in Fluorophores){
    MySecondFunction(BestFluorophore=Name)
}
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
In a way, it is similar to the "if" [conditional](/course/02_FilePaths/index.qmd) we have encountered previously, with "for" being syntax highlighted. Immediately after for we find our parenthesis (), followed by {} that surround individual lines of code. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Within the (), the vector or list is being iterated through is placed after "in" (which also ends up being syntax highlighted). So in this case, we place our vector Fluorophores. 
:::
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
For what is placed before "in", we basically provide an argument that will stand-in for the "i-nth" item in our vector. You will often see "i" used, but you can call it whatever you like, so we will use Name in this case. You will see this "i-nth" standin is then used in place of the value within our line of code present within the {}. 
:::
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Consequently, when we run the for-loop, we get the following outputs:
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#| message: true
for (Name in Fluorophores){
    MySecondFunction(BestFluorophore=Name)
}
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Can these outputs be saved? Yes they can, but some modification will be needed. Messages in R are kind of ephemeral and not saved, we could modify our original function from using `messsages()`to a more permanent `paste0` statement to show-case this in a for-loop. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
MySecondFunctionPartB <- function(BestFluorophore){
    Message <- paste0("Unquestionably, the best fluorophore is ", BestFluorophore)
    Message
}
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Secondly, we would need to save the output to something external that can be retrieved. This can be accomplished by setting up an external list using the `list()` function, and then adding the message to the list for each iteration within the for-loop brackets {} 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#| message: true
MyOutputList <- list()

SavedOutputs <- for (Name in Fluorophores){
    InthMessage <- MySecondFunctionPartB(BestFluorophore=Name)
    MyOutputList[[Name]] <- InthMessage 
}

MyOutputList
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
So as you can see, for-loops are a bit complicated, but can be useful once you get the hang of the structure and syntax. One thing to note, is that they can be slow (especially if you are haven't predefined the list size), which is why opinions on their use will be contested by individual R developers. 
:::
:::

---

### purrr

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
When iterating through a vector or list to pass the values to a function, a commonly used alternative to for-loops are higher-order functions like the `walk()` and `map()` functions from the `purrr` package. These are the tidyverse equivalents to base R `apply()`, `lapply()`, etc. but with less nuanced argument syntax. We will use these frequently throughout the rest of the course. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(purrr)
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Let's first see how these two differ, starting with `walk()`
:::
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#| message: true
walk(.x=Fluorophores, .f=MySecondFunction)
```

:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
As you can see, unlike for-loops, the additional structural elements provided are minimal. Within the (), our Fluorophore vector we want to iterate through is provided to the ".x" argument, while to ".f" we designate the function we want to provide the iterated outputs.
:::
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Let's see how `walk()` differs from its counterpart `map()`
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#| message: true
Outputs <- map(.x=Fluorophores, .f=MySecondFunction)
Outputs
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
In this case, we can see we get both the messages to console, but also individual empty (NULL) entries to a list-style object. This is partly due to the `message()` use we specified above for SecondFunction (which we corrected for with SecondFunctionPartB)
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
If we try to save `walk()` outputs using our modified PartB function, we quickly realize nothing is being saved, with only the input being recorded
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#| message: true
Outputs <- walk(.x=Fluorophores, .f=MySecondFunctionPartB)
Outputs
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
The reason behind this is `walk()` is more behind the scenes, used under circumstances when you want something run, but don't need the return. By contrast, returning things is the main focus of `map()`. 
:::
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Let's see how `walk()` differs from its counterpart `map()`
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#| message: true
Outputs <- map(.x=Fluorophores, .f=MySecondFunctionPartB)
Outputs
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
We will explore how these can be used more extensively in a bit. 
:::
:::

---


## Building Functions

### Background


---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
With this bit of background on general function structure, and a brief introduction to iteration, lets try to write a function that might be conceivably useful for some of us who are still active at the lab-bench. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Our dataset for today is made up of cell count .fcs files. Our lab works [extensively](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/immunology/articles/10.3389/fimmu.2025.1628145/full) with cryopreserved cord blood mononuclear cells, which have large fractions of CD45- cells. Since our primary focus is on T cells, when resting or stimulating the cells we need to be able to calculate the concentration of CD45+ cells for each specimen vial following the thaw so that we can appropiately resuspend the vial to the correct resting concentration of R10 media. 
:::
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
To do this, after thawing and the initial spin, I stain 5 uL of cells for for 7 minutes with a small fluorophore cocktail, consisting of CD45 (APC), CD19 (Pacific Blue) and CD14 (PE). After incubation, cells are resuspended in 500 uL (so a 1:100 dilution), before quickly acquiring on a a 4-laser Cytek Aurora, set to stop collecting after 5000 events are reached. 
:::
:::


::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
If you remember back to [Week 03](/course/03_InsideFCSFile/), we saw that within the keyword/description list a volume recorded measurement was stored. Since we have volume, if we are able to gate for CD45+ (using either `openCyto` or `flowGate`) and retrieve the cell counts, we can then solve for cell concentration (since C1V1=C2V2, etc.) This is then followed by multiplying for dilution (x100), multiplying by total volume, and tada! we have the information that is needed.  
:::
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
After doing this all by hand-for-several years, one of the first things I did after finally having my "it-finally-clicks" moment in R was to automate this process to [get back back reports](https://github.com/DavidRach/AlphaBeta/tree/main/LabNotebook) that look like this, shortening the overall experiment day
:::
:::

---

![](images/CellRestCounts_2025-07-31.png)

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Since this all occurs at the level of a [function](https://github.com/DavidRach/Luciernaga/blob/main/R/Wetlab_Concentration.R), I think sequentially walking through this function creation would be a good bridging component for learning how to write your own functions. Granted, it may be too much, too quick, which is why this session is called "It's Raining Functions!", since it may ultimately viewed as a torrential downpour. 
:::
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
And with that, let's brave the rain. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
![](https://images2.minutemediacdn.com/image/upload/c_fill,w_720,ar_16:9,f_auto,q_auto,g_auto/shape/cover/sport/raining-monkeys-e3fa8001e4eb47433b1cc58e2017d2b8.png){width=75%}
:::

---


### Planning


---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
So based on our initial idea, we know that we will need to get our .fcs files into a GatingSet, which we will then be able to iterate through. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
As we iterate through these files, we will need to retrieve the description/keyword for volume that was encoded by the instrument, as well as any other useful metadata. 
:::
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
And we will need to apply gates (either through openCyto or flowGate), that would allow us to retrieve the counts for various cell populations we might be wanting to retrieve concentration info for. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Let's go ahead and tackle these in turn, re-using code that we have encountered over the last several weeks
:::
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Getting started, as always, we will need to load any libraries we will be using extensively. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
library(flowWorkspace)
library(flowGate)
library(ggplot2)
library(ggcyto)
library(dplyr)
library(purrr)
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
And then we need to identify our fcs files
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
# StorageLocation <- file.path("course", "09_Functions", "data") # Interactive path
StorageLocation <- file.path("data") # Quarto Rendering path

fcs_files <- list.files(StorageLocation, pattern=".fcs", full.names=TRUE)
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Before proceeding to add them into a GatingSet
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
MyCytoset <- load_cytoset_from_fcs(fcs_files, transformation=FALSE, truncate_max_range=FALSE)
MyGatingSet <- GatingSet(MyCytoset)
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
For this particular counting fluorophore panel, only 3 antibodies were used. Since the goal was to quickly acquire the .fcs files before passing off to R, the files were never unmixed, and are still in a raw. fcs format. So we will need to figure out the peak detectors for APC, PE, and Pacific Blue on a 4-laser Cytek Aurora. 
:::
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
# remotes::install_github("DavidRach/Luciernaga")
# install.packages("plotly")
Plots <- Luciernaga::QC_ReferenceLibrary(FluorNameContains="APC$|PE$|Pacific Blue", 
    NumberDetectors=54, returnPlots=TRUE)[[2]]
plotly::ggplotly(Plots)
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
So the equivalent raw detectors we should be paying attention to are going to be V3 (CD19+), B4 (CD45+), and R1 (CD14+)
:::
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Since scale can vary dramatically for individual fluorophores, we might as well and go ahead and transform those three detectors while we are here. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
MainDetectorsOnly <- c("V3-A", "B4-A", "R1-A")

Biexponential <- flowjo_biexp_trans(channelRange=4096, maxValue=262144,
     pos=4.5, neg=2, widthBasis=-500)
MyBiexTransform <- transformerList(MainDetectorsOnly, Biexponential)
transform(MyGatingSet, MyBiexTransform)
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
And a quick-plot check to make sure the transformation looks reasonable enough
:::
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
ggcyto(MyGatingSet[[1]], subset="root",
 aes(x="V3-A", y="R1-A")) + geom_hex(bins=100)
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
ggcyto(MyGatingSet[[1]], subset="root",
 aes(x="V3-A", y="B4-A")) + geom_hex(bins=100)
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
ggcyto(MyGatingSet[[1]], subset="root",
 aes(x="R1-A", y="B4-A")) + geom_hex(bins=100)
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
With that done, let's [add some gates](/course/08_WaysToGate/index.qmd) to separate out CD45+ cells, and below those cells identify CD14+ (mainly monocytes), CD19+ (B cells), and CD14-CD19- (mostly T cells and NK cells for cord blood). My automated version uses `openCyto` with gate constraints to handle the variability, but for this example, lets reuse the `flowGate` approach using `tribble` function for quicker setup. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
GatingTable <- tibble::tribble(
  ~filterId,    ~dims,                          ~subset,           
  "CD45",       list("B4-A", "SSC-A"),            "root",     
  "CD19",       list("V3-A", "SSC-A"),            "CD45",   
  "CD14",       list("R1-A", "SSC-A"),            "CD45", 
  "TandNKcells",  list("V3-A", "SSC-A"),            "CD45",
)
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#| eval: FALSE
flowGate::gs_apply_gating_strategy(MyGatingSet, gating_strategy = GatingTable)
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
And add a general CD45+ gate (i.e. those that I need actual concentration for)
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
![](images/00_CD45.png)
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
And add a general CD19+ gate (B cells)
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
![](images/01_CD19.png)
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
And add a general CD14+ (Monocytes)
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
![](images/02_CD14.png)
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
And add a general gate for likely T and NK cells
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
![](images/03_TandNKprobs.png)
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#| include: FALSE
#| eval: FALSE

# Please ignore this code-chunk, it is used for the website generation to ensure the gated'
# GatingSet is retrieved from storage for use in the subsequent chunks. If you gated via flowGate,
# stick to those. Thanks!

GatingSetStorageLocation <- file.path(StorageLocation, "TheGatingSet.gs")

save_gs(MyGatingSet, GatingSetStorageLocation)
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#| include: FALSE

# Please ignore this code-chunk, it is used for the website generation to ensure the gated'
# GatingSet is retrieved from storage for use in the subsequent chunks. If you gated via flowGate,
# stick to those. Thanks!

GatingSetStorageLocation <- file.path(StorageLocation, "TheGatingSet.gs")

MyGatingSet <- load_gs(GatingSetStorageLocation)
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Ending up with a GatingSet that looks like this. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
plot(MyGatingSet)
```

:::

---


::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
And with that done, let's quickly check the gates using `ggcyto` `autoplot()` function, switching between specimen index values to double check. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
ggcyto::autoplot(MyGatingSet[[6]]) + theme_bw()
```

:::

---


### Building

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Alright, lets start by creating the first layer of our function
:::
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
CellConcentration <- function(){

}
```

:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
At the moment, our function is literally nothing but a wrapper, but when we run the line of code, it is now present under the variables tab. 
:::
:::

---


![](images/04_Variables.png)

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Lets start by considering what we want to do. We have each of our .fcs files currently contained within a GatingSet object. Ultimately, we want to be able to retrieve the cell concentration from each of these files. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
To do so, lets go ahead and plan that we will use the `map()` function to iterate our way through each specimen of the GatingSet object
:::
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
pData(MyGatingSet)$name
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Since when we iterate with `map()` we need to specify a .x argument, let's go ahead and set that as our argument within the (). 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
CellConcentration <- function(x){

}
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
And to make our lives a little easier, we should provide some function documentation as we go, so that future us several months from now can remember what under-caffenaited us today did. In R, this typically is done via what is known as a [roxygen2 skeleton](https://roxygen2.r-lib.org/), which looks like this. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#' This function will figure out the cell concentration
#'  for a given gated population in an .fcs file
#'
#' @param x A GatingSet object (typically iterated in)
#' 
CellConcentration <- function(x){

}
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
In a Roxygen skeleton, we use a modified comment (#') format to identify this as documentation. The start is a generic description for our function. The @ param is used to list the individual argument, and a short description of what it does. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Alright, with that bit of documentation done so that we can contextualize what we did later, let's go back and think mechanics. We will be passing our function through `map()`, so our line of code that we will run will look something like this
:::
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#| eval: FALSE
purrr::map(.x=MyGatingSet, .f=CellConcentration)
```

:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
As map is triggered, we will basically be indexing our way through MyGatingSet, similar to if we ran the lines of code like this
:::
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
MyGatingSet[[1]]
```

:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
MyGatingSet[[2]]
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Let's go ahead and add something inside our function so we can get a readout of what is happening inside the function. Let's use our old-friend `class()` to confirm what is being passed in via `map()`.
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#' This function will figure out the cell concentration
#'  for a given gated population in an .fcs file
#'
#' @param x A GatingSet object (typically iterated in)
#' 
CellConcentration <- function(x){
    class(x)
}
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
purrr::map(.x=MyGatingSet, .f=CellConcentration)
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
So passing through map, we are ending up with invidual "GatingHierarchy" (GatingSet = 1). 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
So with a GatingHierarchy object inside, let's turn to seeing what `flowWorkspace` functions we can use to get our desired outputs out of the description/keyword list. 
:::
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#' This function will figure out the cell concentration
#'  for a given gated population in an .fcs file
#'
#' @param x A GatingSet object (typically iterated in)
#' 
CellConcentration <- function(x){
    keyword(x)
}
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Let's also set `map()` to only target the first specimen in MyGatingSet for now
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
KeywordList <- purrr::map(.x=MyGatingSet[1], .f=CellConcentration)
head(KeywordList[[1]], 20)
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Alright, so we have access to the internal .fcs file keywords, similar to what we saw during [Week 03](/course/03_InsideFCSFile/index.qmd). We can check through the full list and see what might be useful for us for this targeted problem, and within the function isolate them to their own respective variables for subsequent use. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#' This function will figure out the cell concentration
#'  for a given gated population in an .fcs file
#'
#' @param x A GatingSet object (typically iterated in)
#' 
CellConcentration <- function(x){
    Volume <- keyword(x)$`$VOL`
}
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
purrr::map(.x=MyGatingSet[1], .f=CellConcentration)
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
As you can see, since we have changed the internal code within the function, we are now getting back the value recorded for acquired volume when our function is run. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#' This function will figure out the cell concentration
#'  for a given gated population in an .fcs file
#'
#' @param x A GatingSet object (typically iterated in)
#' 
CellConcentration <- function(x){
    Date <- keyword(x)$`$DATE` # Day-Month-Year
    InstrumentSerial <- keyword(x)$`$CYTSN`
    TotalFileEvents <- keyword(x)$`$TOT`
    Specimen <- keyword(x)$GUID
    StartTime <- keyword(x)$`$BTIM`
    EndTime <- keyword(x)$`$ETIM`
    Volume <- keyword(x)$`$VOL`
}
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
purrr::map(.x=MyGatingSet[1], .f=CellConcentration)
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
You will notice however that despite finding all these other keywords, we are only getting back the volume as an output. The reason for this is a function returns by default the last object created within it. We can change this by setting a `return()` value equal to something else. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#' This function will figure out the cell concentration
#'  for a given gated population in an .fcs file
#'
#' @param x A GatingSet object (typically iterated in)
#' 
CellConcentration <- function(x){
    Date <- keyword(x)$`$DATE` # Day-Month-Year
    InstrumentSerial <- keyword(x)$`$CYTSN`
    TotalFileEvents <- keyword(x)$`$TOT`
    Specimen <- keyword(x)$GUID
    StartTime <- keyword(x)$`$BTIM`
    EndTime <- keyword(x)$`$ETIM`
    Volume <- keyword(x)$`$VOL`

    return(InstrumentSerial)
}
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
purrr::map(.x=MyGatingSet[1], .f=CellConcentration)
```

:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
In this case, we are just retrieving individual bits and pieces. Ideally, we will want to cobble these together into a "data.frame" row or column so that we get everything back in a single go, and have a dataset that can then be used for tidyverse filtering, plotting, etc. 
:::
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
One thing to notice,there is a Total Events (TOT) keyword. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#' This function will figure out the cell concentration
#'  for a given gated population in an .fcs file
#'
#' @param x A GatingSet object (typically iterated in)
#' 
CellConcentration <- function(x){
    Date <- keyword(x)$`$DATE` # Day-Month-Year
    InstrumentSerial <- keyword(x)$`$CYTSN`
    TotalFileEvents <- keyword(x)$`$TOT`
    Specimen <- keyword(x)$GUID
    StartTime <- keyword(x)$`$BTIM`
    EndTime <- keyword(x)$`$ETIM`
    Volume <- keyword(x)$`$VOL`

    return(TotalFileEvents)
}
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
purrr::map(.x=MyGatingSet[1], .f=CellConcentration)
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
This however corresponds to every event in the .fcs file, including doublets, debris, and miscellaneous cell populations, nothing specific to our individually drawn gates. 
:::
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
What we can do instead, is use a `flowWorkspace` function we learned about during [Week 05](/course/05_GatingSets/index.qmd), namely the `gs_pop_get_data()` function. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
MySubset <- gs_pop_get_data(MyGatingSet[[1]], "root")
nrow(MySubset)[[1]]
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
The main purpose for `gs_pop_get_data()` is to reach through the pointer and retrieve the data associated with a particular gate. We can then assess this subsetted data using our regular R functions. 
:::
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
You may have noticed the subset argument. We can switch it out for the respective gate names to retrieve the counts for cells present within each. 
:::
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
TheSubset <- gs_pop_get_data(MyGatingSet[1], "CD45")
nrow(TheSubset)
```

:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
TheSubset <- gs_pop_get_data(MyGatingSet[1], "CD14")
nrow(TheSubset)
```

:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
TheSubset <- gs_pop_get_data(MyGatingSet[1], "CD19")
nrow(TheSubset)
```

:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
TheSubset <- gs_pop_get_data(MyGatingSet[1], "TandNKcells")
nrow(TheSubset)
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
As you can see, we can access the counts for the cells found within the respective gate by providing the gate name as the second argument. Let's modify our function, to add subset argument
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#' This function will figure out the cell concentration
#'  for a given gated population in an .fcs file
#'
#' @param x A GatingSet object (typically iterated in)
#' @param subset The gate from which to retrieve cell counts from
#' 
CellConcentration <- function(x, subset){
    Date <- keyword(x)$`$DATE` # Day-Month-Year
    InstrumentSerial <- keyword(x)$`$CYTSN`
    TotalFileEvents <- keyword(x)$`$TOT`
    Specimen <- keyword(x)$GUID
    StartTime <- keyword(x)$`$BTIM`
    EndTime <- keyword(x)$`$ETIM`
    Volume <- keyword(x)$`$VOL`

    EventsInTheGate <- gs_pop_get_data(x, subset)
    Cells <- nrow(EventsInTheGate)

    return(Cells)
}
```

:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
purrr::map(.x=MyGatingSet[1], .f=CellConcentration, subset="CD45")
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Alright, we have now found all the bits and pieces we will likely need to estimate cell concentration. Let's start by making sure that Volume is switched over to numeric (as keywords can often be stored as character values)
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#' This function will figure out the cell concentration
#'  for a given gated population in an .fcs file
#'
#' @param x A GatingSet object (typically iterated in)
#' @param subset The gate from which to retrieve cell counts from
#' 
CellConcentration <- function(x, subset){
    Date <- keyword(x)$`$DATE` # Day-Month-Year
    InstrumentSerial <- keyword(x)$`$CYTSN`
    TotalFileEvents <- keyword(x)$`$TOT`
    Specimen <- keyword(x)$GUID
    StartTime <- keyword(x)$`$BTIM`
    EndTime <- keyword(x)$`$ETIM`


    Volume <- keyword(x)$`$VOL`
    Volume <- as.numeric(Volume)

    EventsInTheGate <- gs_pop_get_data(x, subset)
    Cells <- nrow(EventsInTheGate)

    return(Cells)
}
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Next, remembering our C1V1 = C2V2, we can set up the formula
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#' This function will figure out the cell concentration
#'  for a given gated population in an .fcs file
#'
#' @param x A GatingSet object (typically iterated in)
#' @param subset The gate from which to retrieve cell counts from
#' 
CellConcentration <- function(x, subset){
    Date <- keyword(x)$`$DATE` # Day-Month-Year
    InstrumentSerial <- keyword(x)$`$CYTSN`
    TotalFileEvents <- keyword(x)$`$TOT`
    Specimen <- keyword(x)$GUID
    StartTime <- keyword(x)$`$BTIM`
    EndTime <- keyword(x)$`$ETIM`


    Volume <- keyword(x)$`$VOL`
    Volume <- as.numeric(Volume)

    EventsInTheGate <- gs_pop_get_data(x, subset)
    Cells <- nrow(EventsInTheGate)[[1]] #[[1]] since the output is in a list

    Concentration <- (Cells*1000)/Volume

    return(Concentration)
}
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
And let's check across our specimens:
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
purrr::map(.x=MyGatingSet, .f=CellConcentration, subset="CD19")
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
In the case for our counting tubes, after staining they were diluted 1:100. So we need to add a dilution multiplier to the above. We can set that as our third argument.
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#' This function will figure out the cell concentration
#'  for a given gated population in an .fcs file
#'
#' @param x A GatingSet object (typically iterated in)
#' @param subset The gate from which to retrieve cell counts from
#' @param dilutionMultiplier Multiplies the estimated concentration
#'  to bring correct digits. 
#' 
CellConcentration <- function(x, subset, dilutionMultiplier){
    Date <- keyword(x)$`$DATE` # Day-Month-Year
    InstrumentSerial <- keyword(x)$`$CYTSN`
    TotalFileEvents <- keyword(x)$`$TOT`
    Specimen <- keyword(x)$GUID
    StartTime <- keyword(x)$`$BTIM`
    EndTime <- keyword(x)$`$ETIM`


    Volume <- keyword(x)$`$VOL`
    Volume <- as.numeric(Volume)

    EventsInTheGate <- gs_pop_get_data(x, subset)
    Cells <- nrow(EventsInTheGate)[[1]] #[[1]] since the output is in a list

    Concentration <- (Cells*1000)/Volume
    Concentration <- Concentration*dilutionMultiplier

    return(Concentration)
}
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
purrr::map(.x=MyGatingSet, .f=CellConcentration, subset="CD19", dilutionMultiplier=100)
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
With this calculated, we can now proceed to data tidying to make sure everything important gets returned back to us as an individual row. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#' This function will figure out the cell concentration
#'  for a given gated population in an .fcs file
#'
#' @param x A GatingSet object (typically iterated in)
#' @param subset The gate from which to retrieve cell counts from
#' @param dilutionMultiplier Multiplies the estimated concentration
#'  to bring correct digits. 
#' 
CellConcentration <- function(x, subset, dilutionMultiplier){
    Date <- keyword(x)$`$DATE` # Day-Month-Year
    InstrumentSerial <- keyword(x)$`$CYTSN`
    TotalFileEvents <- keyword(x)$`$TOT`
    Specimen <- keyword(x)$GUID
    StartTime <- keyword(x)$`$BTIM`
    EndTime <- keyword(x)$`$ETIM`

    Volume <- keyword(x)$`$VOL`
    Volume <- as.numeric(Volume)

    EventsInTheGate <- gs_pop_get_data(x, subset)
    Cells <- nrow(EventsInTheGate)[[1]] #[[1]] since the output is in a list

    Concentration <- (Cells*1000)/Volume
    Concentration <- Concentration*dilutionMultiplier

    Data <- data.frame(
        Date=Date,
        InstrumentSerial=InstrumentSerial,
        Specimen=Specimen,
        StartTime=StartTime,
        EndTime=EndTime,
        Volume=Volume, 
        Cells=Cells,
        Concentration=Concentration
        )

    return(Data)
}
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
purrr::map(.x=MyGatingSet[1], .f=CellConcentration, subset="CD19", dilutionMultiplier=100)
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
And we can use the `dplyr` package `bind_rows()` to assemble all our outputs for the individual .fcs files
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
Dataset <- purrr::map(.x=MyGatingSet,
 .f=CellConcentration, subset="CD19", 
 dilutionMultiplier=100) |> bind_rows()
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
Dataset
```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
And now with our dataset complete, we can save it out for later use. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#| eval: FALSE

# StorageLocation <- file.path("data") #Within Working Directory
StorageLocation <- file.path("C:", "User", "FulanoDeTal", "Desktop") #Elsewhere on your computer, customize
StorageLocationAndName <- file.path(StorageLocation, "CellConcentrationsForTodaysExperiment.csv")
write.csv(Dataset, StorageLocationAndName, row.names=FALSE)
```

:::

# Discussion

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
In this session, we survived the downpour that is creating functions in R. We looked at both relatively simple examples (to get an idea of the general syntax) as well as step-by-step built out the cell concentration to get a better fundamental understanding of how some of these more targeted applications take various inputs and arguments, run code using these inputs, and then return us a specified output. We also ventured into iteration and how the purrr package can help us in this regard. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
I would like to re-emphasize that this material **is a lot**, and that feeling like you will need to revisit this topic is to be expected. When I first encountered a for-loop, I didn't know what to do with it. By the time I had figured it out, I couldn't envision how to write a function or iterate through a vector. Figuring out how to do so takes time, and often arises more out of targeted need. You want to do something for your cytometry pipeline, but are tired of copying and pasting with minor changes, you end up generalizing it into a function. 
:::
:::

---

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
I also highly recommend looking at the additional resources, because in the end, learning about the concepts from different perspectives is always helpful, since every instructors focus will differ. 
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip title="."}
Fortunately, now that we have encountered functions and iteration, we will gradually see more examples in context of focused application as we head into the next section. We will start focusing on acquiring some of the toolsets we need for high-dimensional and unsupervised analysis, starting next time with how to downsample events from individual .fcs files, concatenate them together, and write them to a new .fcs file that won't be immediately corrupt on import into your favorite commercial software. 
:::
:::

---

![](images/TakeAway.png)

---

# Additional Resources

[Riffomonas Project: A tutorial for writing functions in R](https://youtu.be/3uK1OzA7CTs?si=XPXoI_ipo7d8P1Hv)

[R for Data Science: Functions](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/functions.html)

[Riffomonas Project: Repeat an R function with the tidyverse, purrr and furrr](https://youtu.be/U7ynho78-vA?si=oJo72HclMdpaW12P)

[Advanced R: Functions](http://adv-r.had.co.nz/Functions.html)

# Take-home Problems

:::{.callout-tip title="Problem 1"}
Modify one of the simpler functions (SecondFunction or similar), provide your own argument names, and modify the `message()`, `paste0` or `print()` functions to print a text style output. Generate a small vector of values, and iterate through your vector using one of the approaches we used.
:::

---

:::{.callout-tip title="Problem 2"}
Using the initial framework for the CellConcentration function, retrieve several other keywords that are of interest, and incorporate them into the returned data.frame row. 
:::

---

:::{.callout-tip title="Problem 3"}
For CellConcentration, we retrieved both start and end time. Look up information on `lubridate` package, convert these times to a time-style format, and from acquired volume derrive uL/min at which each .fcs file was acquired. Did this vary at all across days?
:::

---

![](images/TakeAway.png)

::: {style="text-align: right;"}
[![AGPL-3.0](https://www.gnu.org/graphics/agplv3-with-text-162x68.png)](https://www.gnu.org/licenses/agpl-3.0.en.html) [![CC BY-SA 4.0](https://licensebuttons.net/l/by-sa/4.0/88x31.png)](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/)
:::
